skill.name: slack-gif-creator
skill.description: Create animated GIFs optimized for Slack emoji and messages using PIL, imageio, easing helpers, frame composition tools, and Slack-focused validation guidance.
skill.summary: Create Slack-ready animated GIFs with reusable Python utilities and practical optimization guidance.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported high-risk keywords and many command patterns, but review found these are false positives from animation terminology, Python examples, and markdown code fences. No malicious intent, prompt injection, network access, credential access, or obfuscated execution was found. The remaining risk is low because the utilities read and write local image files as part of GIF creation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography matches occur in prose that describes GIF animation utilities, not cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, or credential handling was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text about GIF creation and animation frames. There is no cryptographic API call or security-sensitive data flow.

low_findings.title: False Positive: C2 Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The C2 matches are local math variables named c1, c2, and c3 inside easing formulas. They do not indicate command and control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variables are constants used in an easing function calculation. The file contains no networking, persistence, command dispatch, or remote endpoint logic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The command findings are mainly fenced Python examples and inline identifiers in documentation. The only shell command shown is a dependency installation instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is documentation and sample code, not hidden execution logic. The dependency install line is explicit and user-visible.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Keywords
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The matches occur in documentation for creating blank frames and imported helper functions. No host inventory, environment probing, or system command collection was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text refers to frame creation and reusable animation helpers. There is no evidence of system information gathering.

low_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Capability
low_findings.description: The skill can load user-provided images and save generated GIF files to local paths. This is expected for a GIF creation tool, but users should choose output paths carefully.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The save method writes to a caller-provided path, and the documentation shows loading a local image. This is legitimate but still a real filesystem capability.

content.value_statement: Teams often need small, clear GIFs that work well as Slack emoji or message reactions. This skill provides practical constraints, Python helpers, and optimization guidance for creating polished Slack-ready animations.
actual_capabilities: Build animated GIFs from PIL images or numpy frame arrays.
Resize and color-optimize GIFs for Slack emoji constraints.
Provide easing functions for bounce, elastic, back, and linear motion.
Draw basic shapes, stars, gradients, and text into animation frames.
Validate GIF dimensions, frame count, duration, and file size guidance.
Document Slack-focused settings for emoji and message GIFs.
limitations: It does not include pre-made graphics or animation templates.
It relies on local Python dependencies such as Pillow, imageio, and numpy.
Emoji font rendering is not reliable across platforms.
Large or complex animations may still need manual size reduction.

use_cases.title: Create Team Emoji Reactions
use_cases.description: Generate small animated emoji GIFs for team celebrations, status updates, or recurring Slack rituals.
use_cases.target_user: Team leads and workplace community managers

use_cases.title: Prototype Product Launch GIFs
use_cases.description: Create lightweight animated message GIFs that announce releases, milestones, or feature updates in Slack.

use_cases.title: Build Custom Visual Assets
use_cases.description: Use Python drawing utilities to create branded motion graphics without relying on prebuilt asset packs.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Emoji GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small Slack emoji.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 128 by 128 Slack emoji GIF of a bouncing star. Keep it under three seconds and optimize it for Slack.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an Uploaded Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a provided image as the visual source.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use my uploaded image as the main subject. Create a short Slack GIF where it gently pulses and rotates.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Message Reaction
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a larger GIF for posting in a Slack channel.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 480 by 480 Slack message GIF for a product launch. Use a clean background, bold motion, and readable text.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize an Existing GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request focused on size and validation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this GIF for Slack readiness, then reduce frames, colors, or dimensions until it is suitable for Slack emoji use.

output_examples.input: Make a Slack emoji GIF of a star bouncing twice and landing with a sparkle.
output_examples.output: A compact 128 by 128 animated GIF plan with bounce easing, twelve frames, a bright star shape, sparkle accents, and Slack emoji optimization.

output_examples.input: Turn this uploaded logo into a celebratory Slack reaction GIF.
output_examples.output: A short animation concept that uses the logo directly, adds a pulse effect, applies a subtle rotation, and exports a Slack-ready GIF.

output_examples.input: My GIF is too large for Slack. Make it smaller without losing the main motion.
output_examples.output: A size reduction strategy using fewer frames, fewer colors, duplicate-frame removal, and emoji dimensions when appropriate.

best_practices: Use 128 by 128 dimensions for Slack emoji GIFs.
Keep emoji animations short and readable at small sizes.
Reduce colors and frames before sacrificing the core motion.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on emoji fonts for consistent rendering.
Do not create long emoji loops with unnecessary frames.
Do not use low contrast text or thin outlines in small GIFs.

faq.question: Can this skill create Slack emoji GIFs?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides dimensions, frame settings, optimization steps, and Python utilities for Slack emoji GIF creation.

faq.question: Can it use an uploaded image?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation shows how to load a local image with PIL and use it directly or as visual inspiration.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made animation templates?
faq.answer: No. It provides utilities and concepts, but the animation logic is created for each request.

faq.question: What Python packages does it need?
faq.answer: It lists Pillow, imageio, imageio-ffmpeg, and numpy as dependencies.

faq.question: Can it validate whether a GIF is Slack-ready?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance and validation utilities for dimensions, size, duration, frame count, and FPS.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed static findings were false positives, and no malicious behavior was found.
